name: Sensacq Loop
name_original: Pimbo et la collégiale Saint-Barthélemy – boucle
description: Hike the moderate 4.2-mile Pimbo and Saint-Barthélemy collegiate church loop, featuring historic sites and views of the Pyrénées.
region_intro: Hiking around Pimbo offers a network of trails through a landscape characterized by rolling hills and rural countryside. The region features moderate elevation changes, with paths winding through agricultural areas and natural settings. Hikers can expect a mix of open views and shaded sections, providing diverse experiences. The terrain is generally accessible, with options ranging from easy strolls to more challenging long-distance routes. Best hiking trails around Pimbo The most popular hiking route is Sensacq Loop, a 4.2 miles (6.8 km) trail that takes 1 hour 51 minutes to complete. This moderate loop offers views of the surrounding rural landscape. Another top favourite among local hikers is Pimbo – Moulin de Louvigny loop from Arzacq-Arraziguet, a difficult 16.2 miles (26.0 km) path. This challenging route traverses varied countryside, including sections near Moulin de Louvigny. Local hikers also love the Compostelle milestone 908km – Moulin de Louvigny loop from Arzacq-Arraziguet, a 5.2 miles (8.4 km) trail leading through rural paths and past local landmarks, often completed in about 2 hours 23 minutes. Hiking around Pimbo is defined by rolling hills, rural paths, and countryside views. The network offers options for different ability levels, from easy walks to more difficult long-distance trails. The routes in Pimbo are highly rated by the komoot community with an average score of 4.5 stars from over 40 reviews. More than 380 hikers have used komoot to explore Pimbo's varied terrain.
region_faq:
Q: How many hiking trails are available in Pimbo?
A: Pimbo offers a diverse network of over 25 hiking trails. These routes cater to various preferences, including easy strolls, moderate loops, and more challenging long-distance paths.

Q: What kind of terrain can I expect when hiking around Pimbo?
A: The region around Pimbo is characterized by rolling hills and rural countryside. You'll find paths winding through agricultural areas and natural settings, with moderate elevation changes. Hikers can expect a mix of open views and shaded sections, providing a varied experience.

Q: Are there easy or beginner-friendly hiking routes in Pimbo?
A: Yes, Pimbo has several easy options perfect for beginners or those looking for a relaxed walk. There are 10 routes classified as easy. While not explicitly listed as easy, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e996618452/sensacq-loop" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Sensacq Loop&lt;/a&gt; is a moderate 4.2-mile trail that offers a good introduction to the area's scenery without being overly strenuous.

Q: Are there any circular walks in Pimbo?
A: Many of the trails in Pimbo are designed as circular routes, allowing you to start and end at the same point. A popular option is the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/24647780" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Compostelle milestone 908km – Moulin de Louvigny loop from Arzacq-Arraziguet&lt;/a&gt;, which is a moderate 5.2-mile trail offering rural paths and local landmarks.

Q: Can I bring my dog on the trails in Pimbo?
A: Generally, dogs are welcome on most rural hiking trails in Pimbo. However, it's always recommended to keep your dog on a leash, especially when passing through agricultural land or near livestock, and to clean up after them. Always check local signage for any specific restrictions.

Q: What interesting landmarks or attractions can I see while hiking in Pimbo?
A: While hiking in Pimbo, you can encounter several points of interest. The &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/3919338" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Moulin de Louvigny&lt;/a&gt; is a notable landmark, and you might also pass by historic religious buildings such as the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/3914509" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Saint-Jacques Church of Sensacq&lt;/a&gt; or the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5345562" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Collegiate Church of Saint Bartholomew&lt;/a&gt;.

Q: What do other hikers say about the trails in Pimbo?
A: The hiking trails in Pimbo are highly regarded by the komoot community, with an average rating of 4.5 out of 5 stars from over 40 reviews. Hikers often praise the peaceful rural atmosphere and the well-maintained paths that offer beautiful countryside views.

Q: Are there any longer, more challenging hikes for experienced hikers in Pimbo?
A: Yes, for experienced hikers seeking a challenge, Pimbo offers several difficult routes. One such option is the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/40991279" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Pimbo – Moulin de Louvigny loop from Arzacq-Arraziguet&lt;/a&gt;, a demanding 16.2-mile path that traverses varied countryside and includes significant elevation changes.

Q: What is the best time of year for hiking in Pimbo?
A: The best time for hiking in Pimbo is typically during spring (April to June) and autumn (September to October) when the weather is mild and the natural scenery is vibrant. Summer can also be pleasant, though it can get warm, while winter walks are possible but may involve cooler temperatures and potentially muddy paths.

Q: Where can I find parking for hiking trails in Pimbo?
A: Parking for hiking trails in Pimbo is generally available in or near the villages that serve as starting points for routes. Look for designated parking areas in village centers or at specific trailheads. It's always a good idea to check maps or local information for the most convenient parking spots for your chosen route.

Q: Are there family-friendly hiking options in Pimbo?
A: Many of the easier and shorter routes in Pimbo are suitable for families. These trails often feature gentle gradients and pleasant scenery, making them enjoyable for all ages. Consider routes that are under 5 miles for a comfortable family outing.

Q: What should I wear for hiking in Pimbo?
A: For hiking in Pimbo, it's advisable to wear comfortable, layered clothing suitable for the weather conditions. Sturdy hiking shoes or boots are recommended due to varied terrain, which can include dirt paths and potentially muddy sections. Always carry water, and consider a hat and sunscreen during warmer months.
tips: This 11th century church is a stopover on the road to Le Puy on the pilgrimage to Santiago de Compostela. Despite its various restorations, it retains the military aspect found in many churches in the region.

faqs.question: How long does it typically take to complete the Pimbo loop?
faqs.answer: This moderate 6.82-kilometer loop generally takes around 1 hour and 50 minutes to complete. However, your actual time may vary depending on your pace and how long you spend enjoying the sights.

faqs.question: Is the Pimbo loop suitable for all fitness levels?
faqs.answer: While classified as 'moderate' with an elevation gain of about 90 meters, the trail is generally accessible and suitable for most skill levels. A good fitness level is recommended, but the paths are mostly manageable, though hikers should remain vigilant on varied surfaces.

faqs.question: What kind of terrain can I expect on the trail?
faqs.answer: The loop offers a diverse mix of surfaces, including paved, natural, asphalt, and unpaved sections. You'll walk on rural roads for about 4.42 km, mountain hiking paths for 1.36 km, and general paths for nearly 1 km, providing a varied walking experience.

faqs.question: What are the main points of interest along the route?
faqs.answer: The primary highlight is the historic Collegiate Church of Saint Bartholomew, an 11th-century church with a military-like appearance, significant to the Camino de Santiago pilgrimage. The picturesque village of Pimbo itself is also a charming stop, and on clear days, you can enjoy views of the Tursan region and the Pyrénées mountains.

faqs.question: Does this loop connect with other long-distance trails?
faqs.answer: Yes, the route also passes through sections of the Via Sancti Martini - Chemin de Saragosse, covering about 70% of the loop.

faqs.question: Where can I park to access the trail?
faqs.answer: Specific parking information isn't detailed, but as the trail is a loop starting and ending in the village of Pimbo, you can typically find parking within the village itself, near the collegiate church or other public areas.

faqs.question: Is this trail dog-friendly?
faqs.answer: Generally, trails in this region are dog-friendly, but it's always best to keep your dog on a leash, especially when passing through village areas, near livestock, or on sections shared with roads. Please ensure you clean up after your pet.

faqs.question: What is the best time of year to hike this loop?
faqs.answer: The trail can be enjoyed during most seasons. Spring and autumn offer pleasant temperatures and beautiful scenery. Summer can be warm, so an early start is advisable. Clear days provide the best chance for views of the Pyrénées.

faqs.question: Are there any permits or fees required for this hike?
faqs.answer: No, there are no permits or entrance fees required to hike the Pimbo and the collegiate church of Saint-Barthélemy loop.

faqs.question: Are there facilities like cafes or restrooms available near the trail?
faqs.answer: The charming village of Pimbo, where the loop starts and ends, is likely to offer basic facilities such as cafes or restrooms. It serves as a refreshing stop, especially after your hike.

faqs.question: Are there any tips for making the most of the hike?
faqs.answer: Consider starting your hike early on a clear day to fully appreciate the potential views of the Pyrénées mountains from various points along the route. Take some time to explore the village of Pimbo and the historic Collegiate Church of Saint Bartholomew, which is a significant part of the Camino de Santiago.
